title: Fernanda Nuñez de Moraes -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Senior Content Editor
meeting_title: Fernanda Nuñez de Moraes -Services Hiring Manager Interview | Senior Content Editor

transcript.text: Thank you.
Hello.
Hi.
Hi, Matthew.
Hi.
How are you?
I'm good.
How are you?
Nice to meet you.
Wonderful.
Nice to meet you as well.
Oh, man.
Okay.
So, yeah, I'd to talk to you a little bit.
You had applied for this position, or at least we're talking about the position of a sort of content manager here at GrowthX.
Thanks.
Thank
I'm happy to answer any questions that you have about football, and then I'd love to ask you just a few questions about your background and what you have done in relation to the role.
But are you familiar with what the role entails?
Have you talked to Amrilia a little bit about it, that sort of thing?
Yeah, yeah, yeah.
I had an initial interview with Marilia, so I'm...
Okay, got it.

And as far as the content manager role, here we are emphasizing and leaning into automated workflows, or at least semi-automated workflows with human intervention for a large amount of the content that we produce for clients.
So what we're looking to hire for this role is, very specifically, people with an editorial mindset.
So perhaps, you know, you've done writing, you've done copy editing.
Thank you.

But you also have acted in an editorial capacity because we view the workflows as sort of the baseline writer and we're working to make sure that they are the best that they can be and improving those and giving them feedback and reinforcement to hopefully make them better over time.

But what we really need in this role is people who can look at the output, understand it, and articulate where it is lacking, where it needs improvement, what edits need to be made to get it up to snuff.

And in the near term, perhaps, you know, getting client work out as an example, we'd love for people to make those edits in real time and adjust them to get them into shape that's good to ship, good to get out there for the client.
However, we also want people thinking architecturally like, hey, I'm doing this once, but if I'm doing it once, we may have to do it again.
So, how do I do it again?

Articulate those instructions about the way that this piece falls short, or does it come together in the way that we hope it would, so that when we produce the next article using the workflows, it hopefully has those instructions and articulations integrated into its process.

We have our overall system, we have a platform internally that we built that does this process of creating article assignments from things like keywords or client input, then creates outlines, rough drafts, polishes those rough drafts, and then produces the final article.
We also have separate researching workflows and fact-checking workflows that we can run on those articles to say, hey, you know, look this over, use accredited sources to fact-check it, etc.
But all of those workflows need to be improved over time.
know, they need examples.

Examination of how they're falling short, articulation of the ways specifically they're falling short on a piece-by-piece or output-by-output basis, and then they will always, to some degree, need customization on a client basis.

So as a CM, can be working on work for a handful of clients and understanding, oh, okay, the workflow is falling short in this specific way for this particular client, so we need to add in some instructions or add some clarity to its process so that it delivers us good output.
To give you a grounded example, that might be like a healthcare company would like us to produce content for them related to their universe, and perhaps they are talking about medical billing.
Let's say that's their area of expertise.
They would like a body of work or content around that that shows that they have authority.
And that they know what they're talking about so that a potential client or potential customer would go, oh, you know, they definitely know what they're talking about.
Maybe I should just turn to them for help with this subject matter.

So for a client like that, we might need, as an example, extra steps in the workflow or custom workflows to fact check according to health care regulations in a particular state, you know, or health care regulations overall in the United States or globally if they function globally.
Whereas another, like a fintech company or some other company might not need those workflows, right?
They might not need that articulation of a problem or those steps to check against particular health care regulations or to make sure terminology is correct or accurate, that sort of thing.
So is that kind of the scope or that you had gotten from Marilia, the feeling that you got, or are you getting something?
Yeah.
Yeah.
Okay, great.

And then I'd love to hear a little bit from you about like your background, especially when it comes to editing output, either from yourself or almost more importantly, editing the output of other writers, because effectively the workflows are other writers, right?
Hey, give us output.
I'd love to hear a little bit of an example about your experience there editing other writers.
Yeah, well, okay.
Currently, I'm working as an editorial lead for Hogarth.
It's a production agency for WPP.
I lead a team of nine editors and writers.
And I'm mostly working within the healthcare industry.
Okay.
I have experience.
a good example I picked then.
Yeah, yeah.
I was like, I was like, oh, yeah.
So I

I oversee day-to-day operations, content quality, content accuracy, so I'm experienced in providing feedback to the team and also developing resources, the resources they need, the processes that make sense for our clients.
I'm currently working with Abbott and Centene, so very in the healthcare industry, different clients, of course, different types of content.
So, yeah, my day is about reviewing and writing myself, developing content myself.
So I'm usually developing content for more complex projects and just reviewing what my team creates.
Got it.
And are you using any automation workflows at all in your content creation process?
At the moment?
No, not at the moment.
Not at the moment.
We are working with an internal tool.
I work mostly with ChatGPT, but we are working with an internal tool.
That's WPP Open.
And it's expected to grow.
So the output by now needs a lot of editing, and it's not as precise as we expected to be.
We're not working with automated content for now.
Yeah, got it.
That makes sense.
But you have experience using ChatGPT and other LLMs to edit or to create ideas or things like that?
I use it for pretty much every step in the process, and I found it very useful to help me with substantiation, just making sure the content is...
is...
FDI compliant.
So that's a big part of how we use it.
Yeah.
And how are you giving ChatTBT or Plot or whatever you're using, how are you giving it the guardrails?
Like, explain to the process that you use to have it validate something like, you know, making sure that a piece of writing is FDI compliant or compliant with a particular set of regulations.
How are you having it help you do that?
Because obviously, as a non-deterministic LLM, it can pull information from anywhere.
So explain to me a little bit about your process of giving it some guardrails and helping you get authentic and correct answers for those questions.
Well, first, I'm thinking about two scenarios.
The first one, I create an agent with brand-specific guidelines and FDA guidelines.
So I can check, I can attach my content already checked and just ask for a full review of the content.
And at the same time, when I need to make sure substantiation is correct and it will be legal and regulatory approved, I just ask the tool to mark up.
I submit the paper and I ask it to mark up the exact place where the content is substantiating my claims.
So I found that's very useful and it's very accurate.
So the tool would just give me this markup, this placement, page 123 lines, da, da, da.
This is accurate and this is correct substantiation because different reasons and I will just make sure I will just go to the paper and.
And I would say 9 out of 10, it's correct.
It needs a review, but it saves me hours, hours of extensive work.
Yeah, that makes sense.
That's great.
Thank you for articulating that.
I think that is a helpful way to think about these things.

I mean, you know, creating an agent or a guardrail or a mini model or anything like that that has like the corpus of data that's driven to a client or you know is accurate and vetted for a client can be very helpful.
We do some of that as well.
We'll do a kickoff period with a client where we ask them a bunch of questions, obviously, and kind of calibrate with them what kind of content or what expectations they have.
And as a part of that process, we ask...
Ask them for any bodies of work or information that they have, like sources of truth that they have.
That could be public government websites, if, for instance, they're a company that deals with regulation.
We have a company that acts as a registered agent, which is something that every business needs to have in every state.

If they operate in the United States or globally, it's a little different, but in every state of the United States, they need to have a registered agent, which basically files business paperwork for them every year, and just to enable them to act as a business in that state.

And so those, you know, that company has, they know exactly where those pages live, like what government websites are accurate and up to date at any given moment, that have the appropriate regulations and steps to register as an agent or whatever regulations govern those on a state-by-state basis.
And so we said, okay, great, that's wonderful.
Can you provide those?
And then we use those to inform the pieces.
So we ingest those as a model or as part of the model or give it a list of these resources and tell it explicitly, don't use any resources outside of these.
And then please generate an article about what it is to be or like what it takes to become a Florida registered agent, you know, and then it produces that content.
And usually it's much more correct than if we would have just asked it without providing any reinforcement or guideline.
Exactly.
Yeah.
Yes.
Yeah.
And then I guess like the big things that are needed for this role almost more than anything else is the ability to articulate editorial feedback.
So when, when we have brought some folks on into this role that have been, I'll just be blunt, we've brought on a lot of people.
Some of them are great.
Some of them are less successful so far, doing our best.
We're a young company, right?
So we're still figuring things out like everybody is.

But what we are finding is that some people are coming from the copywriting world, which we initially thought, oh, you know, we probably need copywriters or people who have done marketing, content, writing before.
And I think to some degree, that's true.
It is useful to have that skill set to understand this because ultimately, that's what we are doing.
We're creating marketing content.
We're producing it for clients.
However, what you've come to realize is that as we build our tool, our internal tool, our platform, we call it ContentOS.
But as we build that platform more and we are able to build more and more sophisticated workflows that generate things like research documents, company background, then outlines, articles, polish.

and fact-checking, et cetera, that the people we need piloting those workflows, utilizing them to create the content, really should have much more of an editorial mindset, which, as you know, having done both, it is the same but different.

It is similar, but not all writers can articulate exactly why or what is missing or why they've made a change or what is needed to get from, you know, here to a goal and then be able to break that down and be very articulate about, okay, this doesn't work because of this structural problem or these, this logical underpinning does not link it to the next, you know, section.

in a logical flow, so you just need to add this link and then now everything will flow and it's, you know, some of them are good at it instinctually and they do a fine job, but they cannot articulate it.

And unfortunately, if they cannot articulate it, they cannot really tell when the workflows are breaking and exactly how they are breaking and how they are not living up to expectations, because like any new tool that anybody's building, they're not going to work perfectly every time.

And so to improve, we need folks who can articulate more specifically, hey, I ran this workflow, I got this output, I was able to go in and rectify this, and this is exactly the ways that it should improve in order for me not to have to do this again, you know, so that the next time I can do different things, next time I can find new ways it's breaking, right, so that we can make it better over time.

And so like, that's kind of a big portion of what we are finding is that, yes, the work is getting the output in shape and ready to ship to a client, and we have plenty of people on staff that are doing a fine job at that now, but we don't have that articulation layer that can say, hey, I ran this workflow, I got really
Relatively poor output from it.
I'm just going to fix it.
I'm going use ChatTPT or handwrite it or whatever and ship it to the client.
But then we're missing that, like, okay, can you tell us exactly how it, why it broke, how it broke, how it could be better?
And it's like, ah, not really.
You know, I just kind of, like, did it.
And I think that's what we're missing.
And that's what is really needed at this layer.
So you also need, I just want to try to kind of paint a picture for you.
So that you understand, like, part of the work is to get the client work done.
That's the job.
But part of the work is also to make sure that you have an appetite for understanding the way that automation works.
Which, if you're using an LLM, you're already using automation to some degree.
But understanding the way it works and then contributing to making it better and articulating exactly the ways that it could be improved.
Or at least taking a stab at it.
Doing that and understanding the mechanics of what is happening so that we can say, oh, okay, we're asking it for brevity and it's providing a single line.
Okay, how can we improve that prompt?
How can we improve that workflow so that it doesn't interpret brevity as one-sentence paragraph, LinkedIn style, you know?
It just requires an additional layer of articulation and manipulation that goes beyond, oh, okay, I ran a workflow and now I'm going to use my skills as a writer to just make it good enough to ship.
You know, that's cool.
That's baseline.
But we really need to come in above that with people that have some agency and have some desire to help those workflows improve.
Okay, okay, that makes sense.
Okay, I think that the way my background helps is that I need to be, when reviewing the.

The content that my team creates, you need to be very analytical, and in order to provide feedback, well, I remember when I first started, I had this, I would just rewrite some of the work, and I would just, I had that instinct.
So I had to learn how to be able to analyze the content, in order to be able to show them, guide them, explain very clearly what's not working, maybe provide an example.
Yes, maybe give them ideas, maybe providing an example of something that they've already, they've written in the past, right?
So they can see, sorry, so they can see their own work, performing better.
Oh, I see.
Yeah.
So, I, the way I think my background,
An alliance to what you're saying is that, yeah, I need to analyze what I'm reading.
I need to understand the thought process of who wrote the piece.
So, yeah, I think that would bring value to what you're saying.
Yeah, I mean, I think one way to look at it is, okay, I can fix this for now.
You know, I can fix it for now.
We can move on and we ship this.
They expected some content this week.
We've got it out.
But the real challenge is, okay, how do we fix it for the future?
How do we take that, the adjustments that were made here and codify them, create a very detailed explanation of why, what's breaking, and then potentially why.
And of course, the real why is up to the engineers.

We don't expect you to actually make the improvements in code, but they need to know, okay, oh, okay, so you're seeing this output, but it really should be this output, and this is your articulation about the difference between these two.
Like, oh, it's more detailed in this way, or it's, you know, the length is an issue because of this, or we asked, you know, the workflow to produce this, it produced that.
We think this is the difference between those two, and then it's up to the engineers to then implement those changes and update those changes.

So I think that that's a, you know, that's an important thing to know about the job, because some people like to do that work, you know, the process work and break it down, and then some people are just very, they don't like it so much, right?
They like to do direct editing, make the changes, make it better, make it improved, and ship it, but that articulation, that labor of explaining why the changes are made, that's like the difference.
So it's important to know.
And then did you have any questions for me about the work, the way we work, anything about process, anything like that?
Happy to answer those.
Yeah, was just curious how a typical day would look like in this role.
Yeah.
There's a couple of ways it might look.
There's a way it might look right now.
And there's a way that we think it will look in the near future.
But I'll try and give you a little blend of both.
So primarily, you know, you wake up, you get your coffee, you take a breath, and you're like, okay, it's time to get going.
And we work asynchronously for the most part.
So some overlap with PT or Pacific time is nice, because there are some meetings and things like that, especially client meetings.
A lot of those happen in Pacific time or Eastern time.
But some overlap is nice.
But most of the work can be accomplished asynchronously.
Generally speaking, we like to be several weeks ahead of client deliverables.
So it's not like you're producing something now that's going to be published at 2 p.m.
or something like that.
Generally, it'll be published a week or two out from when you're actually creating it, which gives us some breathing room and time and scheduling and all of that stuff.
So the work itself does not have to be usually day and date.
It's a little bit more async.
But some overlap helps for meetings and collaboration and things like that.
But when you sit down, you would most likely have a queue of work to accomplish for anywhere between one and two, maybe three clients over the course of the next period of deliverables.
That's a week, week and a half, whatever.
The work would be, generally speaking, either editorial content, templated programmatic SEO, so we would
If have a template, you would use the workflows to generate the changes to each template and then review them, make sure they're right.
You know, what does it take to be a registered agent in Florida?
What does it take to be a registered agent in Delaware?
You know, et cetera.
And then fact check and things like that.
The programmatic SEO part of it is pretty straightforward.
The editorial content is really where the quality levels and improvements need to be done, make them human readable, appropriate to Google.
You know, so SEO ready, human readable, that sort of thing.
So you might sit down and have a handful of articles ahead of you to generate that day.
So you would activate the workflows, you'd log onto our platform, you see the work.
Usually it's Airtable where we organize that.
So you'll see Airtable, you'll see a list, and then you would collaborate with your managing editor and you'd say, Hey, what is the client, what's the most important thing for the client?
Perhaps you...
Come up with a way to prioritize those yourself, just so you know what your workload feels like every day, what works for you.
We have our own systems, but it's totally fine for you to create your own workflow systems.
Then you see, okay, cool.
So let's say we have three articles that I want to get to generate today, and then make sure that they're presentable.
You would grab one of them.
Maybe it's a title or a keyword.
Then you would have a workflow, which has the company brief already inserted into the workflow, or you can insert it yourself.

Then it would have polishing instructions, which would come from feedback from a client, like, oh, they like this tone, or that voice, or don't do this, do that, you know, positive and negative reinforcements.
And so you would run the workflow.
The workflow would generate what's called an assignment.
An assignment is basically like, create an article that does X, Y, Z, right?
That's the assignment.
And then embedded in that assignment would be whatever keyword you gave it.
Or whatever subject you gave it, maybe even the headline, that sort of thing.
And then from that assignment, you'd be able to create an outline using a workflow.
So you would generate an outline, and then you would review that outline.
So you're going to review the assignment, make sure that it's not hallucinating or telling you something that you don't want.
Then you tweet that, run the outline.
You would tweet the outline, make sure that, okay, this has all the major elements.
Oh, you know what?
Don't do this.
They don't need this.
That's covered in a different article, or you know what is missing here in this, right?
So critical thinking, that's why humans are in the loop.
Then you make that adjustment, run that workflow to give you a rough draft.
You could tweak the rough draft a bit, you know, make sure that you like the way it looks.
It's not too skeletal, like too many bullet points in one area, things like that.
Get rid of simple LLM whiskers like the blah, blah, blah, colon, blah, blah, blah.
Like, oh, they love to do that in headlines when you've seen that.
Yeah.
Get rid of some simple things like that.
And then you run the polishing instructions on it, which would be suited to the client.
So like voice and tone, sources for things like statistics or code samples, things like that.
So if, for instance, they were a company that has an API that they want developers to use, they would provide us with their documentation.
And we could say, hey, please, if you're going to put a code block in here, it should come from this documentation, not from somebody else.
Because then it'll end up putting a code snippet from a competitor.
And you know how it goes.
Yeah.
So that's what the polishing instructions do.
And then you would get an output.
And that output would be what I would consider to be maybe a rough draft plus from a writer.
You know?
you're like, give you a rough draft.
It's post outline, know, post draft, post even a little bit of feedback, right?
That'd be the polishing instructions.
And then you would get that.
And then you would look at.

Evaluate it, either choose sections to improve with a workflow, another pass through the workflow, like, hey, take this and give me five paragraphs of more narratively driven instead of bullet points, right?
Things like that, things like that.

And once again, we're totally fine with using automated workflows and LLMs to accomplish these tasks, as long as you, of course, then, you know, are thinking critically from first principles about it, making sure that it actually is germane to the article and good quality.

And you would end up with a piece of output that you would then tell your ME, hey, I've put in the draft in the air table, it's ready for review, and then you would move on to your next piece of content.
In my experience, that process generally takes about a half an hour or so, at the most.
You'll get faster at it as you go.
And then the review of the article itself will vary.
It just depends, right?
It may be a complex topic, which may take you a little longer.

Maybe a very straightforward topic, maybe it's a glossary page, like we just want definitions of terms that we publish, those are usually pretty straightforward, know, either it is or isn't true, you know, and that review of content can take a variable amount of time, but I edited, I just did a content audit across all of our org, and using our tools, plus a little bit of LLM help here and there, I edited about 40 pieces in about two days, so I know it's possible, you know, extremely high volume with our tools, it's not always required, you know, on average, you know, a CM is generating, you know, five or six or seven pieces of content a day, on average, I would guess, and that might go up, hopefully, as the quality of the workflows goes up.
Because if you are really getting very confident,

Especially once you refine the workflow for a client and like given it a lot of polishing and detailed instructions, it can be very helpful to then, you know, make that output a lot closer to what you would consider to be okay to publish, you know.
And it takes time to get there.

I'll give you an idea like what we, for a recent client, we created sort of out of the calibration conversation with the client, we created a writing instructions document, which is basically a refinement document like this.
You see this here?
And so this is basically like writing blog posts for this client.
Okay, here's the content philosophy.

They want to teach, they want to build trust, they want to avoid overt sales pitches, you know, they don't like that, you know, like, no thanks, we don't want to sell people hard, we want to be viewed as an authority.
And I won't go through on this, but you can see it's very detailed about like preferred...
Terms and phrasing, you know, that they want.
They want secret list access or do not use secret list security.
We hate that.
It's, you people in our industry hate that stuff.
And, you know, so on and so forth, right?
It uses sample code, you know, this giving you positive and negative examples of like, hey, this is good.
This is bad.
Key features to discuss.
Writing style and structure.
Use bullet points when presenting key features, benefits, or steps.
Maintain the professional access.
We'll stop.
Yada, yada, yada.
You get the idea.
So this writing instruction, those writing instructions can be fed to our workflow.
So it knows how to write these articles.
And the more details you get here, there's a limit in the context windows and LLMs just have a limit to what they can ingest at a time.
But the more details you get in these steps, the closer your output will be.
So if we can work on our workflows through being able to articulate what's wrong and how we can improve.
We view that gap as like narrowing more and more, which increases your leverage as a creator.
So you're able to say, you know, generate a half dozen articles at once and then maybe take a minimal path at each of them and say, hey, you're pretty good.
You know, I think we're in the bulk of these over to the client.
And that's the goal.
You know, that's that's what we want to do.
Great.
Thank you so much for that.
Sounds great.
Cool, cool.
Well, yeah, thank you.
I have to jump to another meeting, but I really appreciate you taking the time.
I think it's cool.
I think your your work as an editor and the fact that you have worked to edit other people's content and are working with the team is very helpful for a position like this.
This is kind of the profile we're looking for more so than somebody who is a writer has been an individual contributor, because this is like managing a team.
Yeah.
Variable quality, you know, like the word.
We're oh, we're cool.
We're great.
This one's doesn't work, you know, and it's just like managing a team of writers in that way.
So I think that skill set is better applicable to it.
Great.
Well, that's great to hear.
Yeah.
Well, great.
Thank you very much.
Marilia will be in touch maybe to schedule some follow-up interviews and we'll go from there.
Okay.
Thank you so much.
Have a great day.
you.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss the Senior Content Editor role at GrowthX with candidate Fernanda Nuñez de Moraes.](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=65.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Role emphasizes editorial mindset, managing automated workflows, and articulating detailed feedback for improvement](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=169.0)
  - [Fernanda's background as an editorial lead aligns well with the position's requirements](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=449.0)
  - [GrowthX uses a platform called ContentOS with various workflows for content creation](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=258.0)
  - [The ideal candidate can analyze output, provide specific feedback, and contribute to workflow improvements](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1034.0)

## Topics

### Role Overview and Requirements

  - [Content manager role focuses on semi-automated workflows with human intervention](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=147.0)
  - [Emphasis on editorial mindset, not just writing skills](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=169.0)
  - [Need for articulating where output is lacking and how to improve it](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=198.0)
  - [Ability to think architecturally about recurring issues and solutions](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=300.0)
  - [Experience with editing other writers' work is crucial](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=422.0)

### GrowthX's Content Creation Process

  - [Use of internal platform (ContentOS) for article creation](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=258.0)
  - [Workflow steps: assignment creation, outlining, rough drafts, polishing, fact-checking](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=258.0)
  - [Customization of workflows based on client needs (e.g., healthcare regulations)](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=370.0)
  - [Importance of providing specific, actionable feedback to improve workflows](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1034.0)

### Fernanda's Relevant Experience

  - [Currently an editorial lead at Hogarth, managing a team of 9 editors/writers](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=449.0)
  - [Experience in healthcare industry content (Abbott, Centene)](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=463.0)
  - [Familiar with using ChatGPT and other AI tools for content creation](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=547.0)
  - [Skilled in providing feedback and developing resources/processes for clients](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=480.0)

### Typical Workday and Expectations

  - [Asynchronous work with some overlap in PT/ET for meetings](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1403.0)
  - [Managing content for 1-3 clients, usually weeks ahead of publication](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1440.0)
  - [Using Airtable for task organization and prioritization](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1546.0)
  - [Running workflows to generate assignments, outlines, and drafts](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1540.0)
  - [Reviewing and refining content at various stages](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1739.0)
  - [Collaborating with Managing Editors for final review](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1773.0)
  - [Average output of 5-7 pieces of content per day](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1784.0)

### Content Quality and Improvement Process

  - [Creating detailed writing instruction documents for clients](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1901.0)
  - [Continuous refinement of workflows based on feedback and analysis](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1975.0)
  - [Goal to narrow the gap between initial output and publishable content](https://fathom.video/share/qCN13ErJN96zHPBxqPxdHTG9puycotdo?tab=summary&timestamp=1986.0)

## Next Steps

  - Marilia to potentially schedule follow-up interviews
  - Further evaluation of Fernanda's fit for the role based on her editorial experience and team management skills
